---
title: "token_registry.aleo"
subtitle: "Aleo's multi-token standard, with the live mainnet token table."
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status:** Stub. Full chapter coming soon.
:::

## The question

Ethereum has ERC-20: every token is its own contract. Aleo's `token_registry.aleo`
takes a different approach — one contract hosts arbitrarily many tokens
identified by a `token_id: field`. Why? What's the trade-off?

## What this chapter will cover

- The "registry vs standalone" decision: why some tokens (Pondo, Verulink-bridged) live in the registry while others (USDCx, USAD) are standalone
- All 22 functions of `token_registry.aleo` mapped to their use cases
- The `external_authorization_required` flag and the `prehook_*` extension point — how registry tokens add custom compliance without modifying the registry contract
- The verified-live token table:

| Symbol | token_id | Issuer | Notes |
|--------|----------|--------|-------|
| ALEO (Pondo pALEO) | `3443843...22344986field` | Pondo | Liquid-staked ALEO |
| vUSDC | `6088188...25268775field` | Verulink | Bridged USDC |
| vUSDT | `7311977...01831695field` | Verulink | Bridged USDT |
| vETH | `1381601...51428034field` | Verulink | Bridged ETH |

(All four token_ids verified live April 2026.)

- The u128 ASCII-as-big-endian encoding of `name` and `symbol` (the surprising gotcha)
- How to discover new registered tokens (no list-all endpoint exists; you scan `register_token` transitions)

## Interactive viz planned

A registry browser: pick a token_id from a dropdown, see its metadata, see a
timeline of its mint/burn/transfer transitions, see its current supply.

## Why this matters

Most production privacy apps will eventually integrate **at least one**
registry-hosted token. Once you understand the registry's API, supporting
N tokens costs the same as supporting 1.
